title: Dissolved Fe(II/III) colorimetric assay using a plate reader (96-well plate)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"bksv4","text":"This protocol describes the adaption of an Iron assay originally described in Viollier 1999 and Stookey 1970, modified for use on a multi-mode plate reader spectrophotometer (BioTek, Synergy 2, Winooski, VT, USA), using standard 96-well plates for rapid measurements of 1 mL water samples. This assay measures the concentration of both Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions in solution.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":78,"length":13},{"key":1,"offset":96,"length":12},{"key":2,"offset":229,"length":14}],"data":[]},{"key":"19h99","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b3o76","text":"Samples for this assay should be filtered (0.2 µm syringe filter), measured or diluted to 1.20 mL volume and placed in 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes. Care should be taken to avoid oxidation of Fe(II) ions by oxygen in the atmosphere, either by performing the steps quickly under a stream of N2 gas right after sampling or by performing the first step of this assay inside an anaerobic chamber.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":291,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":43,"length":21},{"key":4,"offset":119,"length":28}],"data":[]},{"key":"7g6ru","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"56CC1BDD6D6C11EA8D460242AC110002","url":"https://doi.org/10.1016/S0883-2927(99)00097-9"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"7602EC206D6C11EA834CFFA2389E01FF","url":"https://doi.org/10.1021/ac60289a016"}},"2":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"56CC1B4C6D6C11EA8D460242AC110002","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/product/SIGMA/CLS9017?lang=en\u0026region=US"}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"56CC1B7F6D6C11EA8D460242AC110002","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/product/aldrich/wha67802502?lang=en\u0026region=US"}},"4":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"A86813706D6C11EA834CFFA2389E01FF","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/product/sigma/t9661?lang=en\u0026region=US"}}}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"b8jup","text":"Working principle","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"48d5r","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"44p63","text":"Ferrozine binds to both Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions in solution and turns into a magenta-colored complex (note: Fe(II)-induced complex has ~60x stronger absorbance than Fe(III), thus constituting a larger part of the signal). Maximum absorbance is at λ=562 nm at pH 4-9. To determine the concentrations of Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions, two spectrophotometric absorbance measurements are made, termed A1 and A2, where A1 is an initial measurement and A2 after a reduction step to reduce all Fe(III) to Fe(II) ions.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":247,"length":8},{"style":"sub","offset":392,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":399,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":409,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":442,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a1ojm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7k11f","text":"After measuring A1 and A2, the following system of two equations is solved to find CFeII and CFeIII, the molar concentrations of Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions, respectively:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":17,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":24,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":84,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":94,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"drf5l","text":"  ","type":"align-left","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"82kob","text":"where  , x is the species to be measured,   is the molar absorbance coefficient of the specific complex,  the path length. β can be interpreted as the instrument-specific absorbance coefficient and α is the dilution factor (0 \u003c α \u003c 1) due to the addition of reductant reagent to obtain A2.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":42,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":287,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":6,"length":1},{"key":2,"offset":42,"length":1},{"key":3,"offset":105,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"36vij","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2l268","text":"In order to solve for CFeII and CFeIII, all we need to determine are bFeII, bFeIII, and a (and estimate their uncertainties). We can obtain these values by analyzing a dilution series of standard solutions where the concentrations of Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions are known. A realistic α value (along with its uncertainty estimates) is obtained by doing the reduction step a second time to the standards only, obtaining A3 (even though all Fe(III) has already been reduced). Hence α = A3/A2. All analytical uncertainty estimates can be obtained by regression analysis after this step (usually linear regression, estimating error of the slope), using the same dataset already obtained.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":23,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":33,"length":5},{"style":"sub","offset":70,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":77,"length":5},{"style":"sub","offset":416,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":481,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":484,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2t9ur","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5716e","text":"After obtaining βFeII, βFeIII, and α, we may derive CFeII and CFeIII as the following:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":17,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":24,"length":5},{"style":"sub","offset":53,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":63,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cof4f","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":4,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"9kk4u","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":5,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"6iuu8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6i5tg","text":"Measurement range","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"65a5g","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"608in","text":"The range of measurement of this assay is between 0 - 80 µM Fe(II/III), thus for samples containing a higher amount of dissolved Fe, especially anoxic water samples, dilution is required. For unknown samples, it is useful to add a 10x dilution sample. For samples that may contain elevated Fe, an additional 100x dilution is recommended. An accurate way to perform dilution is by measuring mass instead of volume. A pipette (which measures volume) has about 1-3% accuracy, whereas an analytical balance (0.1-1 mg accuracy) is vastly better. Do not switch pipettes during colorimetric assay procedures and pay careful attention.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":50,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7d50","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"A_1=\\beta_{Fe_{II}} C_{Fe_{II}} + \\beta_{Fe_{III}} C_{Fe_{III}} \\\\ A_2=\\beta_{Fe_{II}} (C_{Fe_{II}} + C_{Fe_{III}}) \\alpha","guid":"9C9E86306DBE11EA834CFFA2389E01FF","id":0}},"1":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"\\beta_x=\\epsilon_x l","guid":"1854C3806DC311EA834CFFA2389E01FF","id":0}},"2":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"\\epsilon_x","guid":"62133E206DC311EA834CFFA2389E01FF","id":0}},"3":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"l","guid":"9F445F906DC311EA834CFFA2389E01FF","id":0}},"4":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"C_{Fe_{II}}=\\dfrac{A_2-\\alpha A_1}{\\alpha (\\beta_{Fe_{II}}-\\beta_{Fe_{III}})}","guid":"C7C7D0406DC411EA834CFFA2389E01FF","id":0}},"5":{"type":"tex_formula","mutability":"","data":{"formula":"C_{Fe_{III}}=\\dfrac{\\alpha \\beta_{Fe_{II}} A_1-\\beta_{Fe_{III}} A_2}{\\alpha (\\beta_{Fe_{II}}-\\beta_{Fe_{III}})\\beta_{Fe_{II}}}","guid":"EF0D0CE06DCB11EA834CFFA2389E01FF","id":0}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"4n575","text":"Samples for this assay should be filtered (0.2 µm syringe filter), measured or diluted to 1.20 mL volume and placed in 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes. Care should be taken to avoid oxidation of Fe(II) ions by oxygen in the atmosphere, either by performing the steps quickly under a stream of N2 gas right after sampling or by performing the first step of this assay inside an anaerobic chamber.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":291,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":43,"length":21},{"key":1,"offset":119,"length":28}],"data":[]},{"key":"d33gr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1j2j","text":"This assay has a limited detection range and therefore dilutions of the sample may be required. Refer to the Guideline section to understand the requirements.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"0DCAB77C6EA311EAA1500242AC110002","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/product/aldrich/wha67802502?lang=en\u0026region=US"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"0DCAB7C56EA311EAA1500242AC110002","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/product/sigma/t9661?lang=en\u0026region=US"}}}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"5simj","text":"Use safety goggles and nitrile gloves when performing the steps outlined in this assay. Dispose of left-over chemicals by evaporating off all liquids inside a chemical hood (96-well plate), or by collecting these liquids in designated chem-waste jars.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}

","mutability":"","data":{"amount":"2.02","guid":"4202A2A06E4111EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":2}},"31":{"type":"amount","mutability":"","data":{"amount":"0.5807","guid":"5925A3B06E4111EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":6}},"4":{"type":"concentration","mutability":"","data":{"concentration":"0.1","guid":"D99F56F06E0D11EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":21}},"5":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"CDC139106DD711EA834CFFA2389E01FF","url":"https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/catalog/product/aldrich/372331?lang=en\u0026region=US"}},"6":{"type":"amount","mutability":"","data":{"amount":"200","guid":"001476806E0E11EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":2}},"7":{"type":"amount","mutability":"","data":{"amount":"1.5416","guid":"BDEBD1406E0D11EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":6}},"8":{"type":"amount","mutability":"","data":{"amount":"0.98494","guid":"C62A1BA06E0D11EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":6}},"9":{"type":"concentration","mutability":"","data":{"concentration":"1.4","guid":"4244A2906E1E11EA97F09DCEE04E7537","id":0,"label":"","unit":21}}}}
steps: Measure absorbance A 1 : A 1 is measured immediately after adding 120 µL of reagent A to 1.2 mL of filtered/filtered-diluted samples or undiluted standard solutions. Mix the mixture well with the pipette by gently pipetting up and down a few times. Load three 200 µL mixtures onto a 96-well plate to make triplicate measurements on the spectrophotometer.

Reduction and measure absorbance A 2 : collect 640 µL of the remaining mixture after step 1, then add 120 µL of reagent B . Mix with the pipette and begin the timer. The solution is allowed to react for 00:10:00 to complete the reduction from Fe(III) to Fe(II). At the 10 min mark, add 40 µL reagent C , mix with the pipette and immediately measure A 2 by loading triplicate 200 µL mixtures on the well plate.

Steps 2 is repeated once only for the standards, from which A 3 is measured and α calculated. In the procedure above α should be close to 0.8. The goal of executing this step is to obtain a realistic α together with estimates of its uncertainty during the assay procedure.
creator: Jian Gong
